questions.q: What is the primary advantage of using React Native for mobile development?
options: Write code once in JavaScript and deploy to both iOS and Android with shared logic
React Native provides better performance than native code in all scenarios
React Native eliminates the need for any platform-specific configuration
React Native is the only cross-platform framework available
questions.explanation: React Native allows developers to share JavaScript code across iOS and Android, reducing development time and maintaining consistency. A misconception is that React Native always delivers native-level performance. Native frameworks like Swift or Kotlin may outperform React Native in graphics-intensive apps.

questions.q: How does Flutter differ from React Native in terms of the programming language?
options: Flutter uses Dart; React Native uses JavaScript
Flutter uses JavaScript; React Native uses Python
Both use Swift for iOS and Kotlin for Android
Both use the same language but different runtime environments
questions.explanation: Flutter is built on Dart and compiles to native code directly, while React Native uses JavaScript and runs on a JavaScript bridge. This architectural difference means Flutter often has a smaller app size and potentially better performance.

questions.q: What is a bridge in React Native, and why is it important?
options: A communication layer that translates JavaScript code to native code at runtime
A physical connection between two devices for synchronization
A database abstraction layer for data management
A build configuration file for Android and iOS
questions.explanation: The React Native bridge enables JavaScript to communicate with native modules and platform APIs. Understanding the bridge is important because crossing it repeatedly can impact performance. A misconception is that the bridge is always a bottleneck for most apps.

questions.q: What is the primary trade-off when choosing a cross-platform framework like React Native over native development?
options: Code sharing and faster development time in exchange for potential performance limitations and reduced access to latest platform features
Cross-platform frameworks are always faster and offer full platform access
Native development is always better regardless of project constraints
There are no trade-offs when using cross-platform frameworks
questions.explanation: Cross-platform frameworks excel at code reuse and faster multi-platform deployment but may lag in adopting new OS features and can have performance constraints. Native development offers maximum control but requires duplicate codebases. Project scope should guide the decision.

questions.q: In React Native, how do you access platform-specific native APIs?
options: Using NativeModules to call native code or third-party libraries that expose native functionality
React Native automatically maps all native APIs to JavaScript
Platform-specific APIs are not available in cross-platform frameworks
Using platform detection to conditionally import different JavaScript implementations
questions.explanation: NativeModules enables JavaScript to invoke native Objective-C (iOS) or Java (Android) code directly. Platform-specific styling and logic can use Platform.select(), but true native functionality requires custom modules. Not all native APIs are automatically available.

questions.q: What is Flutter's approach to cross-platform UI consistency compared to React Native?
options: Flutter renders its own UI widgets instead of using native components; React Native uses native components for each platform
Flutter uses native components; React Native renders custom widgets
Both frameworks render identical UIs without platform differences
Flutter is designed only for iOS; React Native supports multiple platforms
questions.explanation: Flutter draws its own widgets using a graphics engine, providing pixel-perfect consistency across platforms. React Native uses platform-native components, which means iOS buttons look like iOS buttons and Android buttons look like Android buttons. Using native components can provide better platform familiarity.

questions.q: What is a hybrid mobile app, and how does it differ from a native app?
options: A hybrid app uses web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) wrapped in a native shell; a native app is written in platform languages like Swift or Kotlin
A hybrid app provides better performance than native apps
Hybrid and native apps are identical; the terms are synonymous
Hybrid apps can only run on Android
questions.explanation: Hybrid apps (Cordova, Ionic) bundle web code inside a native container, while native apps are written in platform-specific languages. Hybrid apps are easier to develop but generally have worse performance and offline capabilities. They remain viable for certain use cases.

questions.q: How do React Native and Flutter handle navigation between screens?
options: Both provide navigation libraries (React Navigation, Flutter Navigator); different libraries offer different patterns like stack, tab, and drawer navigation
Navigation is automatically handled by the framework without requiring explicit configuration
React Native and Flutter use identical navigation APIs
Navigation is only available through native platform APIs
questions.explanation: React Navigation and Flutter's Navigator provide flexible navigation patterns. React Navigation is third-party but widely adopted; Flutter's Navigator is built-in. Both support multiple navigation paradigms. Choice depends on complexity and team familiarity.

questions.q: What is hot reload in Flutter, and how does it improve development velocity?
options: Hot reload instantly updates the running app with code changes without full restart, allowing rapid iteration during development
Hot reload compiles the entire app on every change
Hot reload is slower than traditional app builds
Hot reload only works for visual changes, not logic changes
questions.explanation: Hot reload preserves app state and injects updated code, enabling developers to see changes immediately. This dramatically speeds up the development cycle. A misconception is that hot reload breaks app state. Stateful changes in code may require a full restart for consistency.

questions.q: When should you choose native development over a cross-platform framework?
options: When the app requires maximum performance, deep OS integration, or access to cutting-edge platform features; or when the codebase complexity is high enough that sharing code provides minimal benefit
Cross-platform frameworks are always the better choice
When deploying to iOS only, native development is mandatory
Native development is only necessary for games
questions.explanation: Native development excels in performance-critical apps, heavy OS integration, and early adoption of new platform features. It becomes cost-effective when targeting a single platform. The decision should balance project scope, budget, timeline, and technical requirements.
